text.title: git-server-workflow Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for git-server-workflow. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Keep source code in `~/workspace/repos/` and deployed runtime in `/srv/project/`. Never run git operations (checkout, pull, merge) in the runtime directory.
rule.rationale: Git operations in the runtime directory can interrupt running services, change files mid-request, and leave the directory in a partially-updated state.

rule.statement: Use `rsync -a --delete` with explicit excludes for `.git/`, `__pycache__/`, `.venv/`, `.env`, and `node_modules/`. Never rsync these directories to the runtime.
rule.rationale: `--delete` removes files from runtime that no longer exist in workspace. Without excludes it would delete the `.venv` managed separately and expose secrets via `.env`.

rule.statement: Deploy shared libraries (SDK, shared models) before services that depend on them. Define an explicit ordered array in the deploy script.
rule.rationale: If a service restarts before the SDK it imports is updated, it starts against the old library code, causing version mismatches until the next restart.

rule.statement: Avoid reinstalling pip dependencies on every deploy. Compare requirements files between workspace and last deployed state.
rule.rationale: Dependency installs add several seconds to every deploy. Checking if requirements changed is instant and can skip the install step in 90% of deploys.

rule.statement: To roll back a service, check out the previous commit (or a tag) in the workspace repo, then re-run the deploy script. Never manually edit files in the runtime directory.
rule.rationale: Rolling back via the deploy script ensures the runtime is brought to a consistent state: correct source files, correct pip dependencies, and a clean systemd restart. Ad-hoc manual edits in the runtime diverge from the workspace and break the next normal deploy.

rule.statement: Tag every production release with `git tag v<major>.<minor>.<patch>` before deploying. Push tags to origin.
rule.rationale: Untagged history requires `git log` forensics to find a safe rollback point. Tags make `git checkout v1.4.1` instant and unambiguous. Tags are also visible in `git log --oneline --decorate`.

rule.statement: Use `git worktree add` to give each parallel agent its own working directory on a separate branch. Never have two agents operate on the same branch simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Two agents on the same branch will overwrite each other's uncommitted changes, cause merge conflicts on push, and produce interleaved commits that are hard to review. Each worktree has its own index and HEAD, so agents are fully isolated at the git level.

rule.statement: On a bare repo server, use a `post-receive` hook to trigger deploys automatically when `main` is pushed. The hook must be idempotent and log output to a file.
rule.rationale: Manual SSH + pull cycles after every push are error-prone and slow. A post-receive hook runs once per push, on the correct branch, with no human step required. Logging to a file allows asynchronous inspection of deploy output without holding the git push connection open.

rule.statement: Use `main` as the single deployable branch. Feature work goes on short-lived branches merged via PR. Never deploy from feature branches to production.
rule.rationale: Deploying from arbitrary feature branches creates ambiguity about what is running in production. A single deployable branch (`main`) means the deploy script always knows what to check out, and the post-receive hook has a single trigger condition.
